what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard item that questions whether the 'host not master' design assumption actually holds, argues that it silently inverts in three ways, and proposes concrete enforcement mechanisms to make it hold.
observations: Item id is item-bd818f7f, title 'P14 · Does "host not master" actually hold — or quietly invert?', author_session ch-3mpkjg3r, channel dragnet-development, type block, source claude_code, state current.
It links to board://item-48cd6801 via part_of.
Created and updated timestamps are identical, indicating initial filing.
The body opens with a strong claim: 'host not master' does not hold by default and inverts silently.
It lists three creeping inversions: write races, schema drift from Obsidian YAML reordering, and dialect accretion of Obsidian-only syntax.
It states the grounding doc lacks a single-writer discipline, version/CAS on writes, and a schema-guard on read.
It concludes that the only surviving architecture is a one-way projection: master → generated read-only Obsidian mirror, with Obsidian never writing canonical data.
It explicitly calls out mobile/sync/phone-driven edits as the environment where the two-way model bites hardest.
standouts: The core argument that 'host not master' inverts not by decision but by timing, schema normalization, and dialect lock-in.
The proposal of a one-way, regenerated, read-only Obsidian mirror as the clean 'lens' architecture.
The identification of missing enforcement: single-writer discipline, version/CAS checks, and schema-guard on read.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history.
Uses markdown headings, numbered sections, and bold emphasis for readability.
Breaks a purely neutral/spec tone by using subjective framing ('My honest read', 'bites hardest', 'the only clean honest answer').
concerns: The argument is asserted without inline evidence, code references, or reproduction steps from this file alone.
The referenced 'grounding doc' and its current contents are not included, so the claimed gaps cannot be verified here.
The proposed enforcement mechanisms are not implemented or specified in detail within this file.
The Obsidian-specific behaviors are treated as universal without qualification.
notes: A skeptical design audit arguing that 'host not master' collapses under co-editing unless enforced by single-writer rules, CAS writes, and a one-way Obsidian projection.
questions: What is the exact definition of 'host not master' in this system?
Which specific grounding doc is being referenced, and which of the proposed enforcement items does it already contain?
What Obsidian-only syntax constitutes 'dialect accretion' in practice?
Who is Tim, and what is the precise mobile/sync/phone-driven setup being referenced?
How would the one-way projection handle mobile edits that the user expects to persist?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to question whether 'host not master' holds; the file does exactly that, but it also answers the question negatively and proposes enforcement.
apparent_themes: single source of truth vs distributed editing
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to push the project to recognize that co-editing with Obsidian undermines the host-not-master principle, and to adopt mechanical enforcement rather than rely on architectural promise.
novelty: It reframes a design principle as an operational invariant that must be enforced in code, and it names mobile/sync/phone-driven edits as the specific high-risk case where the two-way model fails hardest.
contribution: A concrete critique of three failure modes (write races, Obsidian schema drift, dialect accretion) and a candidate enforcement pattern (single-writer/one-way projection, version/CAS checks, schema-guard on read) that a fused system could keep for design review.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard item P14 argues that the 'host not master' design assumption silently inverts through write races, Obsidian YAML schema drift, and dialect accretion, and proposes single-writer discipline, version/CAS checks, and a one-way read-only Obsidian projection as necessary enforcement.
intention_for_embedding: To challenge the current grounding doc's enforcement gaps and advocate for a one-way Obsidian mirror with guarded writes.
